slides.title: Beyond a Single Type ♊️
slides.content:
You aren't restricted to just creating one generic variable `<T>`.

Often, a function or class needs to intricately handle two or more completely independent unknown Types seamlessly!

slides.title: Declaring Multiple Generics
slides.content: You simply declare exactly as many type variables as you need elegantly inside the brackets, traditionally sequentially using `<T, U, V>`.

quizData.question: If you dynamically elegantly forcefully explicitly call `mergePairs<boolean, string>(true, "Hello")`, what rigorously meticulously specifically perfectly accurately explicitly are the precise types assigned logically thoroughly exclusively essentially natively intrinsically to the type variables intelligently natively respectively?
quizData.explanation: TypeScript systematically maps the explicitly passed types sequentially to the generic slots.

slides.title: Tuple Returns 📦
slides.content: In our `mergePairs` example above, we can reliably force the type to be a tuple: `return [first, second] as [T, U]`. This effortlessly guarantees the array perfectly returns `[string, number]`.

slides.title: Tuple Returns Example
slides.content: By coercing the return type slightly, TS knows this is a precise tuple, not a generic array.
